docs.title: Arcadia
docs.text:
Arcadia is a highly expressive conlang built on consistency and clarity—without exceptions.

It follows a VSO (Verb-Subject-Object) syntax and draws inspiration from Romance languages for vocabulary, orthography, and pronunciation. The writing system ensures a 1:1 correspondence between spoken and written forms, making it ideal for machine parsing while maintaining rich expressiveness.

Though this expressiveness introduces a steeper learning curve, the absence of exceptions provides a logical, predictable structure.

docs.title: Overview of Arcadia
docs.text:
Arcadia is designed to be a highly regular language, eliminating irregularities and exceptions. While grammar and syntax innovate beyond traditional linguistic patterns, phonetics, writing, and vocabulary remain intentionally familiar, as these aspects are considered less central to linguistic structure.

The language prioritizes expressive precision, always favoring clarity of meaning over simplicity—ensuring depth without unnecessary complexity.

docs.title: Literal Information Flow
docs.text:
A core principle in Arcadia is the explicit communication of information, avoiding assumptions. For example:

  • In English, "The door was black" suggests the color has changed.
  • In Arcadia, sive la portas neri simply states the door was black, without implying any present change.

This literal structure ensures that meaning is conveyed with precision rather than inferred.

While Arcadia's orthography and vocabulary reflect Romance languages, its grammar and syntax incorporate influences from natural languages and conlangs, driving innovation.

docs.title: Key Features of Arcadia
docs.text:
Arcadia introduces several unique or rare linguistic features, including:

  • Four-person system
  • Timeless tenses
  • Factual conditionals in the past

docs.title: Communication
docs.text: This section provides some common ways to communicate.

docs.title: Existence
docs.text:
In zerita, there are three verbs are used to express existence:

  • exis
  • viv
  • stat

docs.title: exis
docs.text: The verb "exis" is used to express existence in a general sense. It doesn't imply presence or absence, but rather the existence of something.

docs.title: viv and stat
docs.text: The verbs "viv" and "stat" are used to express existence. Their difference depends on the nature of the existence being expressed. The verb "viv" is used to express existence in a dynamic sense, while "stat" is used to express existence in a static sense. For example, "statave e silvis" and "vivave e silvis" both mean "There is a forest". The difference between the two is that "viv" emphasises the dynamic nature of the forest, viewing is as a living organism that grows, lives, and decays while "stat" just states that the forest is there. "stat" can be used for humans, animals, and plants, to express the presence of a person, animal, or plant. Similarly, "viv" can be used for objects like a car, a river, or even a house to express their ever-changing nature.

docs.title: Differences between "viv"/"stat" and "exis"
docs.text:
In zerita, in order to choose between "viv"/"stat" and "exis", you need to consider the following:

  1. Are you talking about presence or absence?

    • If yes, use "viv" or "stat", otherwise use "exis".

  2. Are you using the abstract form?

    • If yes, use "exis", otherwise use "viv" or "stat".

  3. Are you using the definite article, or a demonstrative pronoun?

    • If yes, use "viv" or "stat", otherwise use "exis".

docs.title: Linking verbs
docs.text: Zerita has many verbs that can be thought as the counterparties of "to be" and "to have" It's easier to view them from a zerita viewpoint.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
The first group of verbs is about the properties of a subject. Zerita has three closely related verbs for attributes.

  1. s, the attibutal verb.

    • "s" is used to assign an attribute (permanent or temporary) to the subject.

  2. est, the membership verb.

    • "est" is used to assign the subject to a group.

  3. tot, the tautological verb.

    • "tot" is used for definitions, equality, or tautologies.

It's easier to see there differences with some examples.

  1. save ta uovis blanki.

    • translation: The egg is white.
    • explanation: The egg has it's colour attribute to be white.

  2. estave ta deka pari.

    • translation: Ten is even.
    • explanation: Ten is a member of the even numbers.

  3. totave ta rujis i ta azulis purpuri.

    • translation: Red and blue is purple.
    • explanation: Purple and the combination of red and blue are exactly the same thing.

docs.title: Preference
docs.text:
Zerita has 4 main verbs to express preference. The verbs are "amor" (love), "estim" (like), and the negative ones "hostil" (hate) and "avert" (dislike). The negation can be used with the positive verbs to express a more nuanced meaning.

The order of likeness is as follows:

  • amor: love
  • estim: like
  • ne amor: not love
  • ne estim: not like
  • ne avert: not dislike
  • ne hostil: not hate
  • avert: dislike
  • hostil: hate

The "ne estim" and "ne avert" are used to express a neutral feeling, but still having a slight preference.

The verbs can be used with any person, but outside the first person singular, they are uncommon unless they are in the evidential mood or in a question.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
1. estimevo legevo libraton

    • translation: I like reading books.
    • explanation: Notice that the the book is in the abstract form, "libraton", which means that I like reading books in general, not a specific book.

  2. ku estimevi legevi libraton

    • translation: Do you like reading books?

  3. estimeste legeve libraton

    • translation: She likes reading books.
    • explanation: I have evidence for that, she may have told me or I may have seen her reading a lot.

docs.title: Feelings, preference, intention
docs.text: In Zerita, verbs that express feelings, preference, intentions are considered arrogant when used for someone other than the first-person singular. However, this rule does not apply if the verb is in a question or in the evidential mood.

docs.title: Conditionals
docs.text:
Zerita has a rich conditionals system. Some of the following aren't true conditionals in zerita, but they are listed here, as they are expressed via a conditional in many other languages.

  • Implications, which are used to express a direction implication:
    • If the water reaches 100°C in 1 atm, it boils
  • Definitions, which are used for definition:
    • A number bigger than 1 is called prime, if it's only divisible by 1 and itself
  • Anankastics, which are used to describe a necessity:
    • If you wanna be my lover, you gotta get with my friends
  • Biscuit conditionals, where the the truth of the then clause does not depend on the truth of the if clause:
    • There are biscuits on the table, if you want some
  • Counterfactuals, where the if clause is false or impossible:
    • If I were a rich man, all day long I'd biddy biddy bum
    • If I had studied more, I would have passed the exams
  • Anderson Case, which are used as part of an argument for the truth of its if clause:
    • If Jones had taken arsenic, he would not have shown just exactly those symptoms which he does show.
  • Predictive, where the if clause is a possibility:
    • If we work hard, we will finish the project on time

In zerita, conditionals follow a different paradigm:

A zeritan conditional is a multi-part sentence. There isn't a main and a subordinate sentence, as in most languages, but all parts of them form a sentence with multiple verbs.

The tenses follow a temporal order, ie the tense of the then clause is relevant to the tense of the if clause.

docs.title: Factuality
docs.text:
The first distinction is based on the factuality of the if clause. Is it a possibility or is it counterfactual?

  • counterfactual, is an if clause that is or cannot be true.
    • an alternative past: If I had studied more (but I hadn't and I cannot change that)
    • an impossible situation: If I could land this spacecraft on the sun (but it's not possible)
    • an imaginary situation: If you were here (but you're somewhere else)
  • possibility, is an if clause that can be true
    • a future, unknown event: If I come home early (I may come early, I may not)
    • a "placeholder" case: If n in an even number (it depends on n)
    • an unknown: If you had been here (I don't know if you were, I'm trying to explore whether it's possible)

docs.title: Necessity
docs.text:
The second distinction is the necessity of the if clause, ie whether the if clause is also a necessary condition for the main clause to happen. Anankastics are also expressed in the if clause. As in the other parts of zerita, we use the non-necessary form, unless we want to specifically include it. Essentially, we differentiate between:

  • If I work hard, I will be able to afford a new car

A complete conditional means that if I don't work hard, I won't be able to afford a new car, but an incomplete one indicates that I can still afford a new car, even if we don't work time, but with other means, for example by winning the lottery.

docs.title: Implication strength
docs.text:
The third distinction is the strength of the implication of the conditional. If the if clause is true, how probable is for the then clause to happen? Consider the following sentences:

  • If the water reaches 100°C in 1 atm, it boils
  • If you exercise regularly, you'll live more
  • If I win the lottery, I'll buy a Ferrari

The first is a fact. If the water reaches 100°C in 1 atm, it has no other option that to start boiling. Maybe you can boil it in another way (eg in vacuum), but when the if clause is true then the then clause will necessarily happen.

The second is a strong association but not a necessity; you mean exercise, but not reach an old age from another reason. But the probabilities are with you.

The third one is a weaker association. Winning the lottery may change my priorities, although the most probable outcome is still that I'll buy a Ferrari.

docs.title: If clause variation
docs.text:
Factuality and necessity are included in the "if" particle in zerita. For possible if clauses, the if particle is:

  • ce, to express an implication that the if clause isn't a necessary condition
  • lo, to express anankastics
  • celo, to express both.

For counterfactuals, a "u" precedes the if particle. Some examples will clarify the uses of each case:

  1. uce savo riki, se obvend e auton.

    • translation: If I were rich, I would buy a car.
    • explanation: I may still buy it even though I'm not rich.

  2. ucelo savo riki, se obvend e auton.

    • translation: If I were rich, I would buy a car.
    • explanation: I'm not rich, so I won't.

  3. ulo savo riki, se obvend e auton.

    • translation: Only if I were rich, I would buy a car.
    • explanation: I'm not rich, so I won't, but even if I was I may not have bought one.

Zerita is a language that favours dropping information that isn't necessary for the discussion. In this sense, "celo" and "ucelo" are only to be used when the necessity of the conditional must be conveyed, otherwise "ce" and "uce" are to be used.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
1. ce riduvo, se ridavur.

    • translation: When I laugh, everybody is laughing
    • explanation: When I laugh, can be viewed as "if I'm laughing, regardless of past, present, or future"

  2. ce venovi al ta dome, se al edravi e cenon.

    • translation: If you come home, you'll eat dinner.
    • explanation: The speaker puts "venovi" in the future to specify that it's about a future event. The eat is in the present prospective, as to describe that upon arrival the dinner will start afterwards.

docs.title: Then clause variation
docs.text:
In zerita the then particle is mandatory in conditionals, even when it precedes the if clause. There are the 3 different ways to express then:

  • si, which is used when the conditional an scientific implication.
  • se, which is used when the conditional the implication strength is strong.
  • su, which is used when the conditional the implication strength is weaker.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
1. ce jogavos, se vinko.

    • translation: If we play, I will win.
    • explanation: "si" is reserved for proofs.

  2. ce jogavos, su vinko.

    • translation: If we play, I expect to win.
    • explanation: "su" still means that this is the expected outcome.

docs.title: Else clause variation
docs.text:
In zerita the else clause isn't mandatory in conditionals, and conditionals that are introduced with "est" or "cest" cannot have an else clause.

  • eli, which is used when the conditional an scientific implication. It can only be used with "celo" or "ucelo".
  • ele, which is used when the conditional the implication strength is strong.
  • elu, which is used when the conditional the implication strength is weaker.

docs.title: Negation
docs.text: "ni" is used as the negative particle, but to negate a sentence, or a part of it, "ne" is used.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
1. ni, ne donavi ta libron meim.

    • translation: No, you are not giving the book to me.

  2. donavi ne ta libron meim.

    • translation: You are not giving the book to me.
    • explanation: You're giving me something else.

docs.title: Prepositions
docs.text: All prepositions are always used with the absolutive case.

docs.title: Spatial prepositions
docs.text:
• intra: inside
  • super: above/on top of/on
  • sub: under/underneath
  • apud: against/next to
  • kontra: opposite of
  • trans: through

docs.title: Temporal prepositions
docs.text: • dum: during

docs.title: Spatiotemporal prepositions
docs.text:
These prepositions can be used for time and space. They are also used for other scales in general, eg temperature.

  • in: in/on/at a location or time
  • eks: from
  • ad: to
  • sirkum: around
  • inter: between (exclusive)
  • interim: between (inclusive)
  • uske: until/up to
  • ante: in front of (before)
  • post: behind (after)
  • cui: every (eg every 2 minutes)

docs.title: Instrumental prepositions
docs.text:
• ab: by (agent)
  • per: by the means of
  • kum: with
  • sin: without

docs.title: Causal prepositions
docs.text:
• pro: for (purpose)
  • por: for the benefit of
  • anstata: instead of
  • pret: except for
  • lau: according to
  • opon: against (e.g., against the rules)

docs.title: Relational prepositions
docs.text:
• de: of (inalienable possession)
  • du: of (alienable possession)
  • pri: about

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
1. jogavur puereris in ta dome.

    • translation: All the children are playing in the house.
    • explanation: The house is viewed as a location, so we use "in" not "intra".

  2. ce pluvov, se manerovas intra ta dome.

    • translation: If it rains, we'll stay at home.
    • explanation: The house is viewed as a building, so we use "intra" not "in".

docs.title: Reply Words
docs.text:
Arcadia has three primary reply words, each serving a distinct conversational function:

  • si → "Yes" (affirmative response).
  • ni → "No" (negative response).

  • ack → Neutral acknowledgment—used to show that the listener is paying attention or understands without confirming or denying the statement.

  • Usage of "ack" → Commonly used in dialogue when one wants to indicate understanding but avoid committing to a definitive stance.

docs.title: Generic Reply Words
docs.text: Similar to English words like "nowhere," "somewhere," "anywhere," and "everywhere," Arcadia has structured generic reply words that align with interrogatives.

docs.title: Interrogative-Based Reply Forms
docs.text:
Each interrogative combines with prefixes to generate negative, generic, specific, and collective responses:

Interrogative Negative Unknown (generic) Unknown (specific) Collective ce (who/what) ane (no one/nothing) aye (anyone/anything) eye (someone/something) are (everyone/everything) cuál (which) anuál (none) ayuál (any) eyuál (some) aruál (all) cove (where) anove (nowhere) ayove (anywhere) eyove (somewhere) arove (everywhere) cuando (when) anuando (never) ayuando (anytime) eyuando (sometime) aruando (always) cuót (how much/how many) anuót (none) ayuót (any amount) eyuót (some amount) aruót (all) cume (how) anume (no way) ayume (any way) eyume (some way) arume (every way)

  • Prefix System:

    • an- → Negative form (absence or negation).
    • ay- → Unknown generic (any).
    • ey- → Unknown specific (some).
    • ar- → Collective reference (all).

docs.title: Adjectives
docs.text:
Adjectives in zerita decline exactly the same as nouns do. They are placed after the noun that the describe in order of importance, for example both mean "big new knife":

  • e koltis magnis novis
  • e koltis novis magnis

There is an slight difference in intention. The first means that it's a big new knife, but the second means the knife is noticeably new, and maybe so shiny that you'll notice this first.

The number of the adjective always agrees with the nouns that it modifies:

  • e furkis ete e koklis magnesis

    • translation: a big fork and a big spoon

  • e furkis ete e koklis magnis

    • translation: a fork and a big spoon

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• save ta domis magnis es beli.

    • translation: the big house is beautiful.

  • save ta domis es magni ete beli.

    • translation: the house is big and beautiful.

docs.title: Adverbs
docs.text: Adverbs in zerita have no declension. Adverbs are used to modify parts of the sentence. Sometimes, they are derived by adjectives.

docs.title: Derived adverbs
docs.text:
Any adjective can be turned into an adverb, by using the "-ie" suffix:

  • bon -> bónie, well
  • mal -> málie, badly

The "-ie" suffix moves the stressed syllable once towards the end, but never to itself.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
1. kantaves bónie tas avisesis.

    • translation: The birds are singing well.

docs.title: Determiners
docs.text:
Determiners are words that precede a noun, providing additional information about its reference, quantity, or specificity.

Arcadia recognizes six types of determiners:

  • Articles → Defines specificity: the tree, an apple.
  • Demonstrative determiners → Indicates proximity: this machine, that person.
  • Interrogative determiners → Forms questions: which book, what blanket.
  • Possessives → Expresses ownership: Maria's bag, my bed.
  • Quantifiers → Describes quantity: a few dogs, each child.

  • Numerals → Specifies an exact number: two dolphins, a million dollars.

  • Determiners can be combined, as in "the three musketeers."

docs.title: Determiner Rules in Arcadia
docs.text:
• A determiner is mandatory unless the noun is in the zero, or collective number, where no determiner normally used.
  • Proper nouns do not require a determiner but can take one when specificity needs to be emphasized.

docs.title: Articles
docs.text:
Arcadia has five articles, each serving a distinct grammatical function:

  • Definite (la) → la leon → "The lion" (a specific, known lion).
  • Definite-Anaphoric (le) → le leon → "The lion (we talked about earlier)" (referencing a previously mentioned lion).
  • Indefinite-Weak (ya) → ya leon → "Any lion" (a general reference to any lion).
  • Indefinite-Strong (ye) → ye leon → "A lion (one specific but unknown)" (introducing a singular but particular lion).
  • Collective (ta) → ta leon → "A pride of lions" (encapsulating collective meaning).

docs.title: Usage Rules
docs.text:
✔ Definite article (la/le) can modify indefinite determiners, turning them definite:

  • tre homosis → "three people"
  • las tre homosis → "three specific people"

✔ Indefinite article (ya/ye) can transform proper nouns into common nouns:

  • ye adamis → "a person named Adam"

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
1. convenivo adamon.

    • Translation: "I met Adam."
    • Explanation: Refers to a specific person named Adam that the speaker knows.

  2. convenivo le adamon.

    • Translation: "I met Adam."
    • Explanation: Refers to a specific person named Adam that was mentioned before.

  3. convenivo ye adamon.

    • Translation: "I met a guy named Adam."
    • Explanation: Refers to someone named Adam, without assuming familiarity.

docs.title: Definite Article
docs.text: Number Article Singular la Fractional lad Plural las

docs.title: Anaphoric Article
docs.text: Number Article Singular le Fractional led Plural les

docs.title: Weak Indefinite Article
docs.text: Number Article Singular ya Fractional yad Plural yas

docs.title: Strong Indefinite Article
docs.text: Number Article Singular ye Fractional yed Plural yes

docs.title: Collective Article
docs.text: Number Article Singular ta Plural tas Nullar tan Collective tar

docs.title: Pronunciation Adjustments
docs.text:
✔ If a singular article precedes a noun beginning with the same vowel, it contracts, replacing the vowel with an apostrophe:

  • la → l' (la amigos → l' amicus)
  • le → l' (le energias → l' energias)
  • ya → y' (ya avis → y' avis)
  • ye → y' (ye enginas → y' enginas)
  • ta → t' (ta antecenus → t' antecenus)

This ensures smooth pronunciation while maintaining clarity in speech.

docs.title: Demonstratives
docs.text:
Arcadia has three bound deixis elements, that are never used alone. Instead, they attach to interrogatives to add spatial or temporal deixis, specifying proximity or distance.

The three levels of deixis are:

  • est: Close (near the speaker).
  • ess: Far (visible but distant).
  • tis: Very far or invisible (out of sight, abstract, or distant in time).

Since est, ess, and tis are dependent modifiers, they cannot appear independently in speech or writing. They always require an interrogative base to function correctly.

docs.title: Interrogative Demonstratives
docs.text:
Deixis elements are combined with interrogatives to form precise demonstratives:

  • ce: (who/what)

    • Close: este → "This"
    • Far: esse → "That"
    • Very far/invisible: tise → "The one that's out of my sight"

  • cuál: (which)

    • Close: estuál → "This one"
    • Far: essuál → "That one"
    • Very far/invisible: tisuál → "The one that's out of my sight"

  • cove: (where)

    • Close: estove → "Here"
    • Far: essove → "There"
    • Very far/invisible: tisove → "The place that's out of sight"

  • cuando: (when)

    • Close: estuando → "Now"
    • Far: essuando → "The other time"
    • Very far/invisible: tisuando → "In a distant time"

  • cuót: (how much/how many)

    • Close: estuót → "This much"
    • Far: essuót → "That much"
    • Very far/invisible: tisuót → "That much"

  • cume: (how)

    • Close: estume → "This way"
    • Far: essume → "That way"
    • Very far/invisible: tisume → "That way"

Deictic Expressions

"-ove", "-uando", and "-ume" are not true demonstratives, but are included here due to their close relationship with deictic expressions.

docs.title: Agreement Rules
docs.text:
• When combined with a noun, the demonstrative must agree in number.
  • Demonstratives do not take case markings when used with a noun (to avoid redundancy).
  • When used independently, case marking is mandatory.

docs.title: "ess" vs. "tis" in Temporal Contexts
docs.text:
• "essando" → Used when referring to a time within a lifetime.
  • "tisando" → Used when referring to a more distant time or an abstract concept.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Demonstrative with a noun → No case marking required:

    • este pomos → "This apple"
    • estes pomosis → "These apples"
    • estuál pomos → "This one apple" (choosing among apples)
    • estót aguas → "This much water"

  • Demonstrative as a standalone pronoun → Requires case marking:

    • volavo esseon. → "I want that." (Case marking required)
    • volavo tiseon. → "I want the one that's out of sight."

docs.title: Questions
docs.text:
Questions in Arcadia are formed using a question particle, followed by an affirmative sentence with the unknown part omitted.

  • Yes/no questions →Indicated by rising intonation in spoken Arcadia.
  • Open-ended questions → Use interrogative particles, which combine with prepositions and declensions as expected.

docs.title: Question Particles
docs.text:
Particle English Meaning cu Yes/no question ce Who/what Generic inquiry cuál Which Choice selection cove Where Location-based cuando When Time reference cuót How much/how many Quantity inquiry cume How Manner inquiry

  • Interrogative words can combine with prepositions, allowing expanded meanings:

    • pro ce → "Why"
    • por ce → "For whose benefit"

Handling Unknowns

In Arcadia, asking a question causes affected suffixes to drop:

- **"cuót"** removes **quantity markers**, simplifying nouns.
- **"cuando"** drops **time suffixes** when asking about duration.
- **"ce"** removes **person suffixes** when querying the subject of a verb.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Yes/No Question:

    • cu donavi ta libron meim? → "Are you giving the book to me?"

  • Method Inquiry:

    • cume donavi ta libron meim? → "How are you giving the book to me?"
      • Explanation: Asking about the method—e.g., by hand, by post.

  • Location Inquiry:

  • cove donavi ta libron meim? → "Where are you giving the book to me?"

  • Subject Inquiry:

  • ceim donavi ta libron? → "To whom are you giving the book?"

  • ceon donavi meim? → "What are you giving to me?"

  • Specific Selection:

  • cualon libron donavi meim? → "Which book are you giving to me?"

  • Temporal Reference:

  • cuando donovi ta libron meim? → "When are you going to give the book to me?"

  • Explanation: Implies it is in the future.

  • Quantity Inquiry:

  • cuót libron donavi meim? → "How many books are you giving to me?"

docs.title: Asking for the Subject of the Verb
docs.text:
• Keeps only the known parts, ensuring clarity in reference.
Sentence Translation Explanation ceis donav ta libron meim? "Who is giving the book to me?" No indication about who or how many are involved. cesis donaves ta libron meim? "Who is giving the book to me?" Third-person plural—multiple people, excluding you or me. cesis donavis ta libron meim? "Who among you (plural) is giving the book to me?" Used as a follow-up question when the subject is unclear.

docs.title: Common Questions
docs.text:
• cei seve de ve namis? → "What's your name?"
  • cume savi? → "How are you?"
  • ex cove sevi? → "Where are you from?"

docs.title: Numerals
docs.text:
Arcadia uses two parallel number systems:

  • Decimal system → Commonly used for general counting and calculations.
  • Hexadecimal system → Included for future-proofing, allowing expanded numerical representation.

docs.title: Numbers and Writing
docs.text:
• Hexadecimal notation uses 0-9 and A-F, ensuring no overlap with written words, since Arcadia uses only lowercase letters for textual representation.
  • Numbers are grouped using underscores (_) for clarity and unambiguous readability.

docs.title: Basic Numerals
docs.text:
Arcadia defines core numbers with distinct names in both decimal and hexadecimal systems.

Decimal Hexadecimal Name 0 0x0 nula 1 0x1 uno 2 0x2 dos 3 0x3 tres 4 0x4 tetra 5 0x5 pente 6 0x6 ses 7 0x7 septe 8 0x8 okto 9 0x9 nove 10 0xA deka 11 0xB lona 12 0xC bili 13 0xD san 14 0xE dort 15 0xF piec 16 0x10 anim 100 0x64 sent 256 0x100 cem 1_000 0x3E8 mil 4_096 0x1_000 alf 1_000_000 0xF4_240 milonu 16_777_216 0x1_000_000 bet

docs.title: Larger Numbers
docs.text:
• After 999_999, both systems use powers of 1_000_000 to create names for arbitrarily large numbers.
  • For one-word power formations, Arcadia replaces "ad X potente" with the suffix "-ente".

docs.title: Decimal Power Names
docs.text: Decimal Name 1_000_000 milonu 1_000_000_000_000 dosente milonu (milonu ad dos potente)

docs.title: Hexadecimal Power Names
docs.text: Hexadecimal Name 0x1_000_000 bet 0x1_000_000_000_000 dosente bet (bet ad dos potente)

docs.title: Decimal System
docs.text: Decimal Name 11 deka uno 17 deka ses 91 novedeka uno 16 anim 256 cem 4_096 alf 16_777_216 bet 829 oktosent dosdeka nove 2_419 dos mil tetrasent deka nove 32_004 tresdeka dos mil tetra 699_050 sessent novedeka nove mil pentedeka 78_187_493_530 septedeka okto mil sent oktodeka sept milonu tetrasent novedeka tres mil pentesent tresdeka

docs.title: Hexadecimal System
docs.text: Hexadecimal Name 0x11 anim uno 0x5B penteanim lona 0x33D trescem tresanim san 0x973 novecem sesanim tres 0x7_D04 septe alf sancem tetra 0xAA_AAA dekanim deka alf dekacem dekanim deka 0xF4_240 milonu 0x1_234_567_89A alf doscem tresanim tetra bet pentacem sesanim sept alf oktocem noveanim deka

docs.title: Possessives
docs.text:
Arcadia uses the prepositions "de" and "du" to indicate possession.

  • Possessives function as determiners, appearing before the noun.
  • Possessor-first order → The possessor always precedes the possessed in phrase construction.

docs.title: Alienable vs. Inalienable Possession
docs.text:
• "de" → Marks inalienable possession, where the possessor is an integral part of the possessed.

  • "du" → Marks alienable possession, where the possessor is not inherently connected to the possessed.

  • No possessive pronouns → Instead, possession is expressed with "de" or "du" + pronoun.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Alienable Possession (du)

    • save du me libris magne → "My book is big."
    • Explanation: The book is not inherently a part of the speaker.

  • Inalienable Possession (de)

    • seve de me de file viparenis adamo → "The father of my child is Adam."
    • Explanation: The child's relationship to the father is inherent.

  • Possessive Chaining

    • save ta libris du me → "The book is mine."
    • Explanation: Ownership chain follows possessor-first ordering.

docs.title: Quantifiers
docs.text:
Quantifiers are determiners that indicate the quantity of a noun.

  • Nullar and collective numbers → Naturally make "all" and "none" redundant, simplifying expressions.
  • Usage Restrictions → Quantifiers cannot be used with uncountable nouns, since the number already conveys quantity.

docs.title: Core Quantifiers
docs.text:
Arcadia defines the following quantifiers:

  • poco → "A few" (small but distinct amount).
  • aliame → "Some" (can refer to one or multiple).
  • oliame → "Some" (cannot refer to one, only plural).
  • multe → "Many" (large quantity).
  • cisca → "Each" (individual emphasis).

docs.title: Usage Notes
docs.text:
• "aliame" → Even though it can refer to one, it always takes a plural noun form.
  • "cisca" →
    • Used with singular nouns to highlight individual entities.
    • Used with collective nouns to emphasize both individuals and the group.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Collective Focus (puereris in collective form)

    • edravur puereris e pomon → "Every child is eating an apple."
    • Explanation: The collective noun and verb highlight the group as a whole.

  • Singular Emphasis (cisca with singular noun)

    • edrave cisca pueris e pomon → "Each child is eating an apple."
    • Explanation: The verb and noun remain singular, focusing on individual children.

  • Hybrid Emphasis (cisca with collective noun)

    • edravur cisca puereris e pomon → "Each and every child is eating an apple."
    • Explanation: The collective noun + cisca highlights both individuals and the group.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: In this section, you will find examples grouped by language level. The language levels covered are A1 and A2 at the moment.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
In this section, you will find examples for the A1 language level.

The passages, and the phenomena they focus on, are:

  1. about a boy talking a walk
    • adjectives
  2. about two friends having a good day
    • past tense

docs.title: Passage
docs.text: vidivo veon in ta bazare. emerivi es pomeson. dicivo "salvufur", ma ne audivi meon. poste, konvadivos intra ta parko. risivi, ete parlivos. sive e die bone.

docs.title: Translation
docs.text: I saw you at the market. You bought apples. I said "hello", but you did not hear me. Later, we met at the park. You smiled, and we talked. It was a good day.

docs.title: Breakdown
docs.text:
1. vidivo veon in ta bazare.
    • translation: I saw you at the market.
  2. emerivi es pomeson.
    • translation: You bought apples.
  3. dicivo "salvufur", ma ne audivi meon.
    • translation: I said "hello", but you did not hear me.
  4. poste, konvadivos intra ta parko.
    • translation: Later, we met at the park.
  5. risivi, ete parlivos.
    • translation: You smiled, and we talked.
  6. sive e die bone.
    • translation: It was a good day.

docs.title: Passage
docs.text: save ta solis lumini. pasave e vipueris ad ta fluve. vidave e peskon magni. saltave ta peskis. ridave ta vipueris. vadave ad ta dome laeti.

docs.title: Translation
docs.text: The sun is bright. A boy walks to the river. He sees a big fish. The fish jumps. The boy laughs. He goes home happy.

docs.title: Breakdown
docs.text:
1. save ta solis lumini.
    • translation: The sun is bright.
  2. pasave e vipueris ad ta fluve.
    • translation: A boy walks to the river.
  3. vidave e peskon magni.
    • translation: He sees a big fish.
  4. saltave ta peskis.
    • translation: The fish jumps.
  5. ridave ta vipueris.
    • translation: The boy laughs.
  6. vadave ad ta dome laeti.
    • translation: He goes home happy.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
In this section, you will find examples for the A2 language level.

The passages, and the phenomena they focus on, are:

  1. about a typical day
    • iterative aspect

docs.title: Passage
docs.text: em surgave ta solis cui matine. em kantaves es avisesis apud de me fenestre. em potavo teaton ete edravo panaton pro matincenaton. em ambulave de me vecinis kum de ase kanis ante me dome. in vespere, em jovaves es pueresis intra ta viae. em volaudavo multie misme kanton. em venave tas noktis, ete em apparave tas stellesis.

docs.title: Translation
docs.text: The sun rises every morning. Birds sing near my window. I drink tea and eat bread for breakfast. My neighbour walks with his dog in front of my house. In the evening, children play in the street. I listen to the same song many times. Night comes, and the stars appear.

docs.title: Breakdown
docs.text:
1. em surgave ta solis cui matine.
    • translation: The sun rises every morning.
  2. em kantaves es avisesis apud de me fenestre.
    • translation: Birds sing near my window.
  3. em potavo teaton ete edravo panaton pro matincenaton.
    • translation: I drink tea and eat bread for breakfast.
  4. em ambulave de me vecinis kum de ase kanis ante me dome.
    • translation: My neighbour walks with his dog in front of my house.
  5. in vespere, em jovaves es pueresis intra ta viae.
    • translation: In the evening, children play in the street.
  6. em volaudavo multie misme kanton.
    • translation: I listen to the same song many times.
  7. em venave tas noktis, ete em apparave tas stellesis.
    • translation: Night comes, and the stars appear.

docs.title: Passive verbs
docs.text:
In zerita the base transitive verbs are always active, and describe an action from the subject. There is neither a "take" nor a "buy" verb. The object promotion allows creating such verbs.

  • IO promotion: "re-" prefix

  • DO promotion: "ob-" prefix

  • potavo e akvon.

    • translation: I'm drinking water.

  • repotave e akvis meic.

    • translation: Water is being drunk by me.

  • donavo ta libron veim.

    • translation: I am giving the book to you.

  • redonave ta libris meic veim.

    • translation: The book is being given from me to you.

  • obdonavis ta libron meic.

    • translation: You are receiving a book from me.

docs.title: Reflexivity
docs.text:
Base verbs are also never reflexive or reciprocal. A prefix changes the verb in a reflexive or a reciprocal form.

  • reflexive: "aut-" prefix

  • reciprocal: "kon-" prefix

  • autamores adamo ete maria.

    • translation: Adam and Maria love themselves.

  • konamores adamo ete maria.

    • translation: Adam and Maria love each other.

An important case is the generation of verbs like "to trade", from "to sell".

docs.title: Senses
docs.text:
Base sense verbs are used when the subject has the ability to perform the action. Zerita can generate verbs via prefix that are used when the subject active uses their sense. This is analogous to "to see" -> "to watch".

Zerita also allows creating intrasitive verbs for senses. This is analogous to "to hear" -> "to sound".

  • voluntary action: "vol-" prefix

  • intrasitive: "per-" prefix

  • vidavo e peskis.

    • translation: I see a fish.

  • volaudavo e kanto.

    • translation: I am listening to a song.

  • perolfave ta uovis malie.

    • translation: The egg smells bad.

docs.title: Nouns
docs.text:
Nouns are a foundational element of Arcadia's grammar. They carry many of the language's complexities, making them an essential starting point for understanding Arcadia.

Arcadian nouns have five numbers, and seven cases. The order of affixes follows this structure: gender - base - number - case.

The gender and number of a noun convey semantic meaning, while case functions purely as a syntactic marker.

docs.title: Cases
docs.text:
Arcadia has seven grammatical cases, each marked by a suffix. These cases define the syntactic role of a noun within a sentence.

We will use "pan" (bread) as our example noun.

docs.title: Case System
docs.text:
• Nominative ("-is") → Marks the subject of the sentence: panis
  • Accusative ("-on") → Marks the direct object: panon
  • Dative ("-im") → Marks the indirect object (recipient): panim
  • Ablative ("-a") → Marks the indirect object (donor/source): pana
  • Predicative ("-i") → Used with linking verbs to express attributes: pani
  • Adpositional ("-e") → Used after prepositions: pane
  • Vocative ("-o") → Used for direct address: pano

docs.title: Vowel Merging Rules
docs.text:
If a noun ends in a back vowel (a, o, u), the suffix merges with the vowel:

  • Nominative & Accusative: The suffix vowel is dropped (instead of "luna-is" → "lunas").
  • Other cases: The noun's final vowel is dropped (instead of "luna-on" → "lunon").

docs.title: Full declension table
docs.text: Case/Number Singular Fractional Plural Nullar Collective Nominative plantis plantedis plantesis plantenis planteris Accusative planton plantedon planteson plantenon planteron Dative plantim plantedim plantesim plantenim planterim Ablative planta planteda plantesa plantena plantera Predicative planti plantedi plantesi planteni planteri Adpositional plante plantede plantese plantene plantere Vocative planto plantedo planteso planteno plantero

docs.title: Gender
docs.text:
Arcadia is not a gendered language. Gender has no effect on grammatical rules—it is purely semantic, used only when relevant to specify the gender of an object or person.

Gender is marked by the prefixes "vi-" (male) and "me-" (female). If gender does not need to be specified, the noun remains unmarked.

Using Gender

By default, gender should not be marked unless it is explicitly relevant. For example, when referring to a child, "filio" is used unless specifying "son" (vifilio) or "daughter" (mefilio).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Parent

    • Any gender: pareno
    • Father: vipareno
    • Mother: mepareno

  • Child (offspring)

    • Any gender: filio
    • Son: vifilio
    • Daughter: mefilio

docs.title: Number
docs.text:
Numbers are formed by adding a suffix to the root word. The semantic meaning of the suffix varies depending on whether the noun is countable or uncountable.

  • If the noun ends in a consonant, an "e" is inserted between the base and the suffix.

docs.title: Countable Nouns
docs.text:
Countable nouns are those that can be quantified as discrete units—such as apples, kilograms, and humans. Using "dia" (day), here are the suffixes:

  • Singular: No suffix—used for exactly one item (dia → a day).
  • Fractional (-d) → Represents a quantity less than one (diad → part of a day).
  • Plural (-s) → Used for more than one item, even if not a full integer (dias → days).
  • Nullar (-n) → Expresses absence of the noun (dian → no days).
  • Collective (-r) → Represents the totality of the noun (diar → all days).

✔ Negative quantities take the same suffix as their positive counterpart.

docs.title: Uncountable Nouns
docs.text:
Uncountable nouns do not have discrete units—such as water, money, and love. Using "amore" (love), the suffixes and their meanings are:

  • Singular: No suffix—used when quantity is unspecified (amore → love).
  • Fractional (-d) → Represents a small quantity (amored → a little bit of love).
  • Plural (-s) → Expresses a large quantity (amores → an abundance of love).
  • Nullar (-n) → Indicates absence (amoren → no love).
  • Collective (-r) → Represents the totality of the concept (amorer → all love in existence).

docs.title: Pronouns
docs.text:
Arcadia categorizes pronouns into four distinct types:

  • Personal pronouns – Used for referring to individuals in speech and writing.
  • Impersonal pronouns – Express general or indefinite subjects.
  • Demonstrative pronouns – Indicate specific objects or concepts in relation to space or discourse.
  • Interrogative pronouns – Used to ask questions about identity, quantity, selection, or characteristics of a noun.

docs.title: Impersonal Pronouns
docs.text:
Arcadia has two impersonal pronouns, "le" and "alé", which differ in degree of specificity.

  • "le" → Represents an implied but relatively specific vague subject (e.g., a known general group).
  • "alé" → Represents a fully indeterminate or diffuse subject (e.g., society as a whole).

docs.title: Impersonal Pronoun Declensions
docs.text: Both "le" and "alé" follow standard noun declension patterns.

docs.title: le (Specific Impersonal)
docs.text: Case/Number Singular Fractional Plural Nominative leis ledis lesis Accusative leon ledon leson Dative leim ledim lesim Ablative lea leda lesa Predicative lei ledi lesi Adpositional le lede lese Vocative leo ledo leso

docs.title: alé (Vague Indeterminate Impersonal)
docs.text: Case/Number Singular Fractional Plural Nominative aleis aledis alesis Accusative aleon aledon aleson Dative aleim aledim alesim Ablative alea aleda alesa Predicative alei aledi alesi Adpositional alé alede alese Vocative aleo aledo aleso

docs.title: Personal Pronouns
docs.text: Arcadia recognizes three core personal pronouns, corresponding to first, second, and third persons. The fourth person serves a generalized reference, expressing all objects or none—it is handled through indefinite pronouns, rather than a distinct personal form.

docs.title: Pronoun Overview
docs.text: Person Pronoun 1st me 2nd ve 3rd asé

docs.title: Full Declension Table
docs.text: Personal pronouns follow the standard declension patterns of nouns.

docs.title: 1st Person ("me")
docs.text: Case/Number Singular Plural Plural (inclusive) Nominative meis mesis emesis Accusative meon meson emeson Dative meim mesim emesim Ablative mea mesa emesa Predicative mei mesi emesi Adpositional me mese emese Vocative meo meso emeso

docs.title: 2nd Person ("ve")
docs.text: Case/Number Singular Plural Nominative veis vesis Accusative veon veson Dative veim vesim Ablative vea vesa Predicative vei vesi Adpositional ve vese Vocative veo veso

docs.title: 3rd Person ("asé")
docs.text: Case/Number Singular Fractional Plural Nominative aseis asedis asesis Accusative aseon asedon aseson Dative aseim asedim asesim Ablative asea aseda asesa Predicative asei asedi asesi Adpositional asé asede asese Vocative aseo asedo aseso

docs.title: Orthography of Arcadia
docs.text: Arcadia uses 23 letters from the Latin alphabet, excluding k, q, and w. Each letter has a fixed pronunciation, ensuring a consistent spelling-to-sound correspondence.

docs.title: Letters & Pronunciation
docs.text: Letter IPA English Approximation a /a/ hat b /b/ aback c /k/ kite d /d/ dog e /e/ set f /f/ fish g /g/ get h /h/ honey i /i/ ship j /d͜ʒ/ jam l /l/ lamp m /m/ man n /n/ not o /o/ cross p /p/ pan r /r/ run s /s/ sun t /t/ tap u /u/ blue v /v/ van x /ks/ tax y /j/ yes z /z/ zoo

docs.title: Digraphs
docs.text:
Arcadia also uses the following digraphs:

Digraph IPA English Approximation ch /t͜s/ chair dh /ð/ there th /θ/ thanks

Rule

The phoneme /ks/ is always spelled "x", never as "cs."

Pronunciation

Each letter is pronounced according to its IPA value and may differ slightly from English pronunciation. The English equivalent provides only the closest approximation (based on Received Pronunciation).

docs.title: Accentuation
docs.text:
Arcadia uses the acute accent (´) to mark stress:

  • Words without an acute → Default stress on the penultimate syllable (or the only syllable if the word is monosyllabic).
  • Words with an acute → Stress falls on the marked syllable.

Examples:

  • homis (human, person) → stress on "o" (homis).
  • ni (no) → stress on "i" (ni).
  • asé (third-person pronoun) → stress on "é" (asé).

docs.title: Vowel Merging & Macron Notation
docs.text:
When compounding words or adding affixes, identical adjacent vowels are never allowed—they always merge into a single instance.

Rule

In dictionary entries, grammar books, and syntax guides, vowel mergers can be marked with a macron over the vowel (e.g., mē instead of me). However, macrons are not used in normal writing—words are spelled without them in everyday text.

docs.title: Punctuation & Formatting
docs.text:
Arcadia has no capitalization and no silent letters. It uses the following punctuation marks:

Mark Name Usage . Period Ends a declarative sentence. ; Semicolon Connects related independent clauses or separates items in a complex list. , Comma Separates elements in a list or clauses within a sentence. : Colon Introduces a list, quotation, or explanation. ! Exclamation Mark Expresses strong emotion or a command. ? Question Mark Marks a question. "" Quotation Marks Encloses direct speech, quotations, or titles.

docs.title: Hyphens & Apostrophes
docs.text:
• Hyphens (-) → Used for compound words forming a single lexeme.
  • Apostrophes (’) → Used for contractions.
  • Single Quotes (') → Can substitute apostrophes for easier typing.

Punctuation & Quotes

Punctuation should be placed outside quotation marks unless both the quoted sentence and the main sentence end simultaneously.

docs.title: Proper Nouns & Loanwords
docs.text:
• Proper names retain their original spelling (if in Latin alphabet).
  • Names from other scripts use standard Latin transliteration, or a phonetic adaptation.
  • Even with original spelling, names are pronounced as close as possible to the source language.
  • As a reader's aid, a name can be transliterated inside double colons the first time it appears:
    • Example: "mike::maic::"
  • Loanwords may contain the letters k, q, or w, even though they do not appear in native Arcadian words.

docs.title: Verbs
docs.text: Arcadia's verbs dictate sentence structure, shaping main syntax based on verb type. They convey extensive grammatical information through a prefix and suffix system, integrating mood, tense, aspect, and person distinctions.

docs.title: Core Verb Features
docs.text:
• Verb types → Define sentence structure and determine syntactic dependencies.
  • Moods → Arcadia has 8 moods, allowing precise expression of intent and modality.
  • Tenses → Arcadia distinguishes 5 tenses, structuring actions across time frames.
  • Aspects → With 9 aspects, verbs encode action flow and duration.
  • Persons → Due to nullar and collective numbers, Arcadia accommodates 4 grammatical persons.

docs.title: Aspect
docs.text: In zerita there are 9 aspects, which are used to indicate how the verb's action extends over time. In zerita the aspect is expressed by an aspect word, that precedes the verb. The aspect word doesn't have a meaning by itself, but it's a part of the verb.

docs.title: Parts of the aspect
docs.text:
The first part of the aspect word is similar to perfect tenses in English:

  • Perfect: "il", used for actions that have finished
  • Prospective: "al", used for actions that haven't started yet

The second part of the aspect word is concerned with the continuity:

  • Continuous: "um", used for actions run continuously
  • Iterative: "em", used for actions that run on intervals, or as a habit

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• potivo e akvon:
    • I drank water
  • il potivo e akvon:
    • I had drunk water
  • ol potivo e akvon:
    • I was about to drink water
  • um potivo e akvon:
    • I was drinking water
  • ilum potivo e akvon:
    • I had been drinking water
  • olum potivo e akvon:
    • I was about to start drinking water
  • em potivo e akvon:
    • I had the habit of drinking water
  • ilem potivo e akvon:
    • I had had the habit of drinking water
  • olem potivo e akvon:
    • I was about to start having the habit of drinking water

docs.title: Veritas
docs.text: Person/Number Singular Fractional Plural Nullar Collective 1st suvo suvos 1st (inc) suvas 2nd suvi suvis 3rd suve suved suves 4th suvun suvur

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Person/Number Singular Fractional Plural Nullar Collective 1st sevo sevos 1st (inc) sevas 2nd sevi sevis 3rd seve seved seves 4th sevun sevur

docs.title: Present
docs.text: Person/Number Singular Fractional Plural Nullar Collective 1st savo savos 1st (inc) savas 2nd savi savis 3rd save saved saves 4th savun savur

docs.title: Past
docs.text: Person/Number Singular Fractional Plural Nullar Collective 1st sivo sivos 1st (inc) sivas 2nd sivi sivis 3rd sive sived sives 4th sivun sivur

docs.title: Future
docs.text: Person/Number Singular Fractional Plural Nullar Collective 1st sovo sovos 1st (inc) sovas 2nd sovi sovis 3rd sove soved soves 4th sovun sovur

docs.title: Mood
docs.text: In zerita, moods are split the the moods in two groups, the main and the secondary. The main moods cannot be combined between them, but they can be combined with secondary moods. Unless combined, moods in zerita are formed by changing the consonant of the tense suffix.

docs.title: Indicative mood
docs.text: The indicative mood is used to express facts, and it's using the consonant "v".

docs.title: Imperative mood
docs.text: Imperative mood is used to express commands, and it's using the consonants "nt".

docs.title: Obligative Mood
docs.text:
The obligative mood expresses necessity or obligation, and it's using the consonant "d". It's also used to express duty, destiny, or social obligation. Duty and social obligation use the veritas tense, and destiny uses the simple future.

Negating a verb in the obligative mood means that you don't have the obligation, rather than meaning you must not perform the action.

docs.title: Permissive Mood
docs.text: The permissive mood expresses the action of the verb is permitted, and it's using the consonant "m".

docs.title: Potential Mood
docs.text: The potential mood expresses the ability or possibility to do something, and it's using the consonant "p".

docs.title: Secondary moods
docs.text: Secondary moods can be combined. Combination works by adding extra tense suffixes.

docs.title: Optative Mood
docs.text: The optative mood expresses wishes, desires, or hopes, and it's using the consonant "f".

docs.title: Dubitative mood
docs.text: The dubitative mood expresses doubt or scepticism about the action, and it's using the consonant "r".

docs.title: Evidential mood
docs.text:
Evidential expresses different levels of evidence about the action. The following consonants are used:

  • st: for direct evidence, eg when the speaker has seen the action
  • sm: for hearsay
  • sp: for inference

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
1. legavi ta libron.

    • translation: You are reading the book.

  2. leganti ta libron.

    • translation: Read the book!

  3. legadi ta libron.

    • translation: You must read the book.

  4. legami ta libron.

    • translation: You may read the book.

  5. legapi ta libron.

    • translation: You can read the book.

  6. legafi ta libron.

    • translation: May you read the book.

  7. legari ta libron.

    • translation: I don't believe that you're reading the book.

  8. legasti ta libron.

    • translation: You are reading the book.
    • explanation: I have direct evidence about it.

  9. legasmi ta libron.

    • translation: You are reading the book.
    • explanation: Someone told me about it.

  10. legaspi ta libron.

    • translation: You are reading the book.
    • explanation: I inferred that you're doing so, from your other actions.

  11. mortudur vihomeris.

    • translation: All men must die. (valar morghulis)

  12. servudur vihomeris.

    • translation: All men must serve. (valar dohaeris)

  13. legimari ta libron.

    • translation: I doubt that you were allowed to read the book.

docs.title: Person
docs.text:
In Zerita, there are 4 persons: 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and 4th. The 4th person is used to describe total cases, such as zero and collective numbers. Additionally, the 1st person plural has two forms: inclusive and exclusive.

The person and number suffixes are:

Person/Number Singular Fractional Plural Nullar Collective 1st -o -os 1st (inc) -as 2nd -i -is 3rd -e -ed -es 4th -un -ur

Zerita is a pro-drop language, so the pronouns aren't necessary, and should only be used if emphasis is required.

docs.title: Timeless tenses
docs.text: Zerita has two timeless tenses, the veritas tense, and the status.

docs.title: Veritas
docs.text: The veritas tense is used to describe a timeless truth, a scientific fact, or an unchanging state, anything that is true regardless of past, present, or future. In the indicative mood, it's formed using the "-uv" suffix.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: The status tense is used to describe something regardless of past, present, or future, but it's not necessarily lasting forever. In the indicative mood, it's formed using the "-ev" suffix.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
1. glakuve akvatis in 0°C.

    • translation: Water freezes at 0°C.
    • explanation: As a scientific truth, it cannot change.

  2. edrevo postcenaton post cenate.

    • translation: I always eat dessert after dinner.
    • translation: This is something that can change.

docs.title: Timed tenses
docs.text:
There are 3 timed tenses, and their suffixes are:

  • Present: "-av"
  • Past: "-iv"
  • Future: "-ov"

docs.title: Type
docs.text: Zerita has a rich type system for verbs, each one having it's own syntactic rules. The syntax of each sentence stems from the verb and its type. The basic syntax is VSO. In cases that the sentence has a topic, it is placed directly after the verb.

docs.title: Linking verbs
docs.text:
Linking verbs are verbs that don't have an action, but rather they describe an state or a characteristic, for example "I am a doctor" or "The roses smell nice". Syntax in zerita is verb - subject - attribute, where the attribute is in the predicative case.

  1. olfaves tas rosesis amaresi.

    • translation: The roses smell sweet.

One important case in the linking verbs, is introducing oneself, as in "I'm Jon Snow".

  1. seve de me namis jon snow

    • translation: I'm Jon Snow.

docs.title: Impersonal verbs
docs.text:
Impersonal verbs are verbs that describe the state of the world, of a place, or even a time period. For example they describe things like "it snows", and they lack subject and the personal suffix.

  1. nivav.

    • translation: It is snowing.

docs.title: Intransitive verbs
docs.text:
Intransitive verbs are verbs that describe an action without a recipient, for example "the birds are singing", or, verbs that describe an state, "the cat is sleeping". They follow the syntax verb - subject, and the subject is in the nominative case.

  1. kantaves es avisesis.

    • translation: Birds are singing.

docs.title: Transitive verbs
docs.text:
Transitive verbs are verbs that describe an action that has a recipient, for example "I'm drinking water". They follow the syntax verb - subject - object, the subject is in the nominative case, and the object in the accusative. The subject can be dropped, especially in the first, second, and fourth person.

  1. potavo e akvon.

    • translation: I'm drinking water.

docs.title: Distransitive verbs
docs.text:
Distransitive verbs are verbs that describe an action with two recipient, for example "I'm giving the book to you". They follow the syntax verb - subject - direct object - indirect object, the subject is in the nominative case, and the direct object in the accusative and the indirect one is in the dative case. The subject can be dropped, especially in the first, second, and fourth person.

  1. donavo ta libron veim.

    • translation: I am giving the book to you.

  2. donavo ta libron ta filim.

    • translation: I am giving the book to the child.

docs.title: Object promotion
docs.text:
Zerita allows both the direct and the indirect object to be promoted to subject. This happens with the verb derivation system.

  • IO promotion: "re-" prefix
  • DO promotion: "ob-" prefix

After the promotion, the promoted argument is in nominative, and the demoted one in ablative.

  1. potavo e akvon.

    • translation: I'm drinking water.

  2. repotave e akvis meic.

    • translation: Water is being drunk by me.

  3. donavo ta libron veim.

    • translation: I am giving the book to you.

  4. redonave ta libris meic veim.

    • translation: The book is being given from me to you.

  5. obdonavis ta libron meic.

    • translation: You are receiving a book from me.

docs.title: Reflexivity
docs.text:
In zerita reflexivity and reciprocality are handled with prefixes. A prefix changes the verb in a reflexive or a reciprocal form.

  • reflexive: "aut-" prefix

  • reciprocal: "kon-" prefix

  • autamores adamo ete maria.

    • translation: Adam and Maria love themselves.

  • konamores adamo ete maria.

    • translation: Adam and Maria love each other.

docs.title: Vocabulary
docs.text: This section provides a structured overview of Arcadia's vocabulary, categorized for clarity.

docs.title: Categories
docs.text:
• Adjectives
  • Nouns
  • Verbs
  • Connections
  • Adverbs
  • Colours

Each category is linked to its respective section for detailed reference.

docs.title: Opinion
docs.text: Arcadia English beli beautiful boni good mali bad

docs.title: Size
docs.text: Arcadia English magni big pici small lungi long brevi short

docs.title: Age
docs.text: Arcadia English veteri old novi new

docs.title: Light Intensity
docs.text: Arcadia English lumini bright

docs.title: Temperature
docs.text: Arcadia English caldi hot frigidi cold

docs.title: Emotion
docs.text: Arcadia English laeti happy

docs.title: Wealth
docs.text: Arcadia English rici rich pauperi poor

docs.title: Taste
docs.text: Arcadia English dulchi sweet achidi sour amari bitter salini salty sabri umami

docs.title: Adverbs
docs.text: Arcadia English ane nothing este this are everything anove nowhere estove here arove everywhere anondo never estondo now arondo always

docs.title: Colours
docs.text:
Arcadia recognizes 13 base colors, each with a distinct archetypical hue.

Arcadia English HEX Sample nero black #000000 MMMMMMMM blanco white #FFFFFF MMMMMMMM griso grey #808080 MMMMMMMM flavo yellow #FFFF00 MMMMMMMM aranco orange #FF8000 MMMMMMMM sango red #FF0000 MMMMMMMM verdo green #008000 MMMMMMMM ciano cyan #00FFFF MMMMMMMM azulo blue #0000FF MMMMMMMM purpuro purple #800080 MMMMMMMM bruno brown #804020 MMMMMMMM rozo pink #FF80C0 MMMMMMMM beijo beige #F5F5DC MMMMMMMM

docs.title: Modifiers
docs.text:
Arcadia includes two adjectives for shade:

Arcadia English lumo light scuro dark

and four adjectives for color finish:

Arcadia English metallico metallic matto matte gloso glossy neono neon

Shade always precedes the color finish.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
1. azulis glosis

    • translation: glossy blue

  2. sangis scuris

    • translation: dark red

  3. verdis lumis mattis

    • translation: light matte green

docs.title: Articles
docs.text: Arcadia English ta the e a/an

docs.title: Spatial prepositions
docs.text: Arcadia English intra inside super above/on top of/on sub under/underneath apud against/next to contra opposite of trans through

docs.title: Temporal prepositions
docs.text: Arcadia English dum during

docs.title: Spatiotemporal prepositions
docs.text: Arcadia English in in/on/at a location or time ex from ad to circum around inter between (exclusive) interim between (inclusive) usce until/up to ante in front of (before) post behind (after) cui every (e.g., every 2 minutes)

docs.title: Instrumental prepositions
docs.text: Arcadia English ab by (agent) per by the means of cun with sin without

docs.title: Causal prepositions
docs.text: Arcadia English pro for (purpose) por for the benefit of anstata instead of pret except for lau according to opono against (e.g., against the rules)

docs.title: Relational prepositions
docs.text: Arcadia English de of (inalienable possession) du of (alienable possession) pri about

docs.title: Question particles
docs.text: Arcadia English cu yes/no question ce who/what cuál which cove where cuando when cuót how much/how many cume how

docs.title: Numbers
docs.text: Arcadia English nula zero uno one dos two tres three tetra four pente five ses six septe seven octo eight nove nine deca ten

docs.title: Quantifiers
docs.text: Arcadia English poco a few mem same aliame some (can be one) oliame some (cannot be one) multe many cisca each

docs.title: Replies
docs.text: Arcadia English si yes ni no ne not ack acknowledgment

docs.title: Connections
docs.text: Arcadia English sed but i and o or (inclusive) ox or (exclusive) nox nor

docs.title: Conditionals
docs.text: Arcadia English (u)che, (u)lo, (u)chelo if si, se, su then eli, ele, elu else

docs.title: Greeting
docs.text: Arcadia English salvufur hello (initial) salvadur hello (reply)

docs.title: Basic pronouns
docs.text: Arcadia English me I ve you (singular) asé he/she/it

docs.title: Basic nouns
docs.text: Arcadia English amore love agua water bazar market caro car domu house fenestra window fuego fire homo human instructo documentation libri book namo name via street parco park porta door puero child (young human) vecino neighbour
